segments.s139-g97: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning 'when'. این گفته شد is a passive/clausal phrase ('this was said'), where شده is the auxiliary/past marker. مژده دادش is the main action 'gave him the good news' with the enclitic object -ش = 'him'. The prepositional phrase به رخش ('to Rakhsh' / 'toward Rakhsh') is positioned here but functions as the destination for the imperative that follows in the next segment (i.e. the reported command 'go to Rakhsh').
segments.s139-g98: برو is the imperative 'go' (a command addressed to someone). شادمان شد is a predicative phrase 'became joyful' (شادمان = joyful; شد = became). دل تاج‌بخش is the possessed noun 'the heart of the crown-giver/sovereign' and appears after the verb phrase — a common poetic order where the predicate precedes the possessed subject.
segments.s139-g99: بیامد is the simple past 'he came'. بمالید here is a past action 'he rubbed/polished' (the b- prefix is a common classical/poetic form). و links to the next action; زین برنهاد is a verb phrase 'he placed the saddle on' (زین = saddle; برنهاد = put on/placed upon). The three verbs narrate successive actions.
segments.s139-g100: شد is the copular verb 'became' (past). The construction شد از رخش رخشان literally 'became, from Rakhsh, radiant' means 'Rakhsh became radiant'; similarly the parallel phrase و از شاه شاد means 'and the king became glad'. The pattern 'از + noun + adjective' is used here to state that that noun assumed the described quality.
segments.s139-g33: هرآنکس که ... بیند is a protasis ('whoever/whosoever') followed by a present-tense verb used generically. The phrase گرز تو (mace + your) places the possessor after the noun (typical Persian word order in poetry). به چنگ is a prepositional phrase 'in/into [the] grasp' modifying بیند ('sees ... in hand').
segments.s139-g34: This clause has an elliptical or verbless, resultative force: بدرد here functions as a causative/violent verb 'to wound/tear' (poetic usage). دل شیر و چنگ پلنگ are direct objects in parallel (the heart of the lion and the claw of the leopard), joined by و. The verb is understood to apply to both objects.
segments.s139-g35: چو here means 'when' (temporal). برهنه modifies تیغ (an unsheathed or 'bare' sword); تو is the possessor after the noun (poetic order). بیند is present tense 'sees' and عقاب is the subject 'the eagle' that performs the following action.
segments.s139-g36: نیارد is an archaic/poetic third-person form (here negative in sense 'does not / will not bring/perform'), governing the verbal phrase 'به نخچیر کردن شتاب'. The structure به + verbal-noun (نخچیر کردن) + شتاب = 'haste to do [X] / hurry to make [X] prey'; thus the clause means 'it does not hasten to make it prey.'
segments.s139-g37: نشان کمند (the mark/token of the lasso) appears before the verb دارد ('has/possesses'); تو attaches as the possessor of کمند ('your lasso'). دارد is third-person singular present 'has', and هژبر is the subject/possessor's name (proper name placed after the verb in this poetic word order).
segments.s139-g38: ز here means 'because of / from'. بیم سنان تو is a genitival phrase 'the fear of your spear' (بیم + سنان + possessor تو). خون بارد is a compact clause where بارد is a poetic present of باریدن ('rains/falls'), so 'the cloud rains blood' — ابر is the subject placed at the end for emphasis.
segments.s139-g39: چو = 'when'. این داستانها is the object 'these tales' and شنیدم is first-person singular past 'I heard'. ز تو 'from you' indicates the source (I heard them from you). This is a simple temporal clause introducing the speaker's reaction.
segments.s139-g40: بسی is an intensifier 'much/often' here. لب به دندان گزیدم is an idiom ('to bite one's lip') where گزیدم is past first-person of گزیدن meaning 'I bit'. ز تو repeats the cause/source 'because of you' or 'on your account'. The whole clause means 'I repeatedly bit my lip because of you.'
segments.s139-g1: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. The phrase 'یک بهره از تیره‌شب در گذشت' is a past-time clause: 'یک بهره' = 'a portion', 'از تیره‌شب' = 'of the dark night', and 'در گذشت' is past tense 'passed' (3rd person singular). Word order places the temporal clause before the main clause.
segments.s139-g2: Subject 'شباهنگ' (nightingale) precedes the verb. 'بر چرخ گردان' is a prepositional phrase 'upon the revolving sky/wheel' modifying the verb, and 'بگشت' is a past tense form meaning 'turned' or 'returned' (classical preverb b- + گشت).
segments.s139-g3: Here the gerund/masdar-like 'سخن گفتن' ('speaking/words') functions as a noun phrase and is the subject of 'آمد' (past 'came'). 'نهفته به راز' is a postpositive phrase: 'نهفته' = 'hidden' and 'به راز' = 'in/with secrecy' (adverbial).
segments.s139-g4: 'در خوابگه' = 'in the sleeping-place/bedchamber'. 'نرم' functions adverbially 'gently/softly' before the verb, and 'کردند' is past plural 'they did/made'; in this context 'کردند باز' means 'they opened (it)'. 'باز' here is the complement meaning 'open'.
segments.s139-g5: 'یکی' before the noun is the indefinite 'a certain'. 'بنده' = 'servant'. The noun phrase 'شمعی معنبر به دست' is a cluster of modifiers: 'شمعی' (candle-related), 'معنبر' (a stand/platform), and 'به دست' = 'in hand' — together describing what the servant held.
segments.s139-g6: 'خرامان' is an adverb/adjectival adverb meaning 'with a stately/proud gait' placed before the verb. 'بیامد' (classical/formal past of آمدن) = 'came'. 'به بالین مست' is a prepositional phrase 'to the bedside of the drunk' ('بالین' = bedside/pillow, 'مست' = the intoxicated one).
segments.s139-g7: 'پس پرده اندر' = 'behind/after the curtain within' (classical word order: پس + noun + اندر). 'یکی ماه روی' is an indefinite nominative: 'a moon-faced one' (ماه + روی = moon-faced / beautiful-faced). The phrase clusters the locative and the descriptor before the following simile.
segments.s139-g8: چو introduces a simile 'like/as'. 'خورشید تابان' = 'the shining sun'. 'پر از رنگ و بوی' is a predicate/adjectival phrase meaning 'full of color and scent' ('پر' = 'full (of)' followed by the preposition 'از' and the nouns 'رنگ' and 'بوی').
segments.s139-g73: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. خورشید تابان is noun + postpositive adjective ('the shining sun'). ز is the poetic preposition 'from' and چرخ بلند means 'the lofty sky' (چرخ = sky/heaven, بلند = high); the whole phrase sets a temporal/location frame: 'when the shining sun rose from the high heavens'.
segments.s139-g74: همی is an older/progressive auxiliary indicating ongoing or imminent action ('was on the point of/kept on'). خواست is past tense 'wanted/was about to'. افگند is the (classical) stem of افکندن 'to throw/cast' and here follows خواست to express 'was about to cast' or 'wished to cast'. رخشان کمند is the object phrase 'a radiant snare/noose'.
segments.s139-g75: به here functions locatively ('on' / 'upon') introducing location. بازوی رستم is a genitive construction 'the arm of Rostam'. یکی مهره places the numeral before the noun ('one bead/amulet'), and بود is the past copula 'there was'. The clause literally: 'On Rostam's arm one amulet was.'
segments.s139-g76: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'which'. آن مهره repeats the demonstrative 'that amulet'. اندر جهان uses the older preposition اندر = 'in/within' + جهان 'the world'. شهره بود is predicative: شهره (here adjectival 'famous') + بود 'was' = 'was famous'.
segments.s139-g77: بدو is the clitic + pronoun 'to him/unto him'. داد is simple past 'gave'. و links the two actions. گفتش is گفت + enclitic pronoun 'he said to him'. که introduces reported speech/command. این را uses the demonstrative + object marker (را) to mark 'this' as definite object. بدار is an imperative meaning 'keep/hold' (classical imperative form).
segments.s139-g78: اگر introduces a condition 'if'. دختر آرد is a verbal clause where آرد (present/habitual form of آوردن) means 'brings' — 'brings a daughter'. ترا is the dative/accusative 'to you' (object pronoun). را here reinforces definiteness/emphasis (common in classical word order). روزگار 'fate/fortune' is the grammatical subject performing the action; notice the poetic inversion that places روزگار after the object phrase.
segments.s139-g79: بگیر and بدوز are two imperatives: 'take!' and 'sew!' (or 'fasten!'). بگیسوی is a prepositional/adverbial compound (بـ + گیسو) meaning 'toward the tress / to the hair'; او is the third-person pronoun 'her'. بر before بدوز functions as 'on/onto' (directional onto the hair). The sequence gives two commands connected by و.
segments.s139-g80: به here means 'with/under' in the sense of 'by virtue of' (used with stars/omens). نیک اختر is a noun + adjective 'good/auspicious star'. و links the two auspices. فال گیتی فروز is 'an omen (فال) that is giti-froz: gیتی 'world' + فروز ('illuminating' / 'that lights'); فروز is postpositive/attributive, so 'an omen that lights the world' or 'world-illuminating omen'.
segments.s139-g17: چنین is an adverb meaning 'thus/so'. داد is the past tense of دادن ('gave') and here forms the light-verb construction دادِ پاسخ = 'gave an answer' (poetic word order places the verb before its noun). که introduces reported speech/quotation ("that" / "that is:"), and تهمینه‌ام is the name تهمینه plus the enclitic -ام = 'I am', so the clause literally reads 'thus gave answer that I am Tahmineh' (i.e. 'she answered thus: "I am Tahmineh"').
segments.s139-g18: تو گویی here uses گویی (a present/subjunctive-form verb 'you say/you seem to say') addressed to the listener; in classical/poetic Persian گویی often has the sense 'you say' or 'you seem to say'. که introduces the content clause explaining what is being said. از غم is a prepositional phrase 'out of/from grief', به before دو نیمه‌ام functions as 'into / to two halves' (poetic idiom meaning 'split in two'), and نیمه‌ام is نیمه + enclitic -ام = 'I am (in) two halves'.
segments.s139-g19: یکی here means 'one / a certain' (introducing a single person), دخت = 'daughter/maiden', and شاه سمنگان is the genitive 'king of Samangan' (noun + place name). منم is من + enclitic -م 'I am' (colloquial/poetic fused copula). The phrase order is typical of epic diction: 'I am a daughter of the king of Samangan.'
segments.s139-g20: ز (poetic form of از) = 'from/of' indicates origin. پشت here means 'back/behind' used metaphorically as 'lineage/descent' (i.e. 'from the house/back of'). هژبر is a personal name (ancestor), و = 'and', پلنگان literally 'the leopards' (used here as an epithet or clan-symbol), and منم repeats 'I am' (من + enclitic -م). The whole segment states descent/origin: 'I come from the house of Hezbar and of the leopards.'
segments.s139-g21: به گیتی is a locative phrase 'in the world' (poetic use of به for location), ز خوبان = 'from/among the beautiful/fair ones' (ز = from), مرا is the object (م + را combined in the transcribed words) but here functions within the existential negation, جفت نیست = 'there is no match/mate' (جفت 'mate/pair', نیست 'is not'). The clause means 'in the world there is no match for me among the beautiful.'
segments.s139-g22: چو = 'as/like' used for comparison ('as for one like me'), من = 'me/I'. زیر چرخ کبود is a fixed poetic locution 'under the blue/azure sky' (زیر = under, چرخ = sky/heaven, کبود = dark-blue/azure). اندکیست is a fused existential phrase meaning 'there are few' or 'it is little' (poetic; اندک + است contracted). The clause therefore says 'there are few like me under the blue sky.'
segments.s139-g23: کس here in the negative context functions as 'no one'. از پرده بیرون = 'out from behind the curtain/veil' (از = from/out of, پرده = curtain/veil, بیرون = outside/out). ندیدی is the negative past form 'has not seen' (classical/poetic placement; the subject 'no one' governs the negative), and مرا (م + را) marks the object 'me'. Together: 'No one has seen me out from behind the veil.'
segments.s139-g24: نه هرگز = 'nor never / never' (negator + adverb), کس again = 'anyone/no one' (here continuing the negative sense), آوا = 'voice/sound', شنیدی = 'has heard' (past; here in negative sense: 'has not heard'), and مرا = object-marker 'me'. The clause means 'nor has anyone ever heard my voice.'
segments.s139-g65: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بسپرد is an archaic perfect/past-form of سپردن 'to hand/entrust' (they handed over). دختر is the direct object 'the daughter'. ب + دان here spell out the phrase 'به آن' (to that), i.e. 'to that', and پهلوان is the indirect-object 'hero'. Word order is S (implied agent) V O IO (typical older Persian ordering).
segments.s139-g66: همه is the subject 'everyone'. شاد گشتند is a two‑word verbal phrase: شاد (happy) + گشتند (past plural of گشتن used here 'became'), so 'became happy'. پیر و جوان is a paired expression 'old and young' appended as an appositive (i.e. everyone, both old and young).
segments.s139-g67: ز شادی ('from/because of joy') gives the reason. بسی is an adverb 'much' / 'a great deal of'. زر is the direct object 'gold'. برافشاندند is the past plural verb 'they scattered/threw up' (celebratory scattering of coins). The whole clause means 'because of joy they scattered much gold.'
segments.s139-g68: آفرین خواندند is a fixed expression 'they cried “afarin” (bravo/praise)' where آفرین is the acclamation and خواندند is 'they called/shouted'. پهلوان between them is the addressee ('hero') — literally 'they called “Bravo, hero!”'. ابر is unclear/archaic here and does not carry the literal sense 'cloud' in ordinary syntax; it functions as a poetic epithet or is anomalous in the manuscript.
segments.s139-g69: که introduces the quoted wish/utterance. این ماه نو 'this new moon' is the subject; بر تو is the prepositional phrase 'for you' (lit. 'upon you'). فرخنده is an adjective 'auspicious/fortunate'. باد is the optative auxiliary particle (old Persian/poetic) meaning 'may it be' — together giving the blessing 'May this new moon be auspicious to you.'
segments.s139-g70: سر بدسگالان تو is a genitival phrase: سر 'head' + بدسگالان 'ill‑omened / ill‑fated (ones)' + تو 'your' (possessive). کنده is a past participle 'uprooted/severed' and باد again is the optative 'may it be', so the clause expresses a curse/wish: 'May the heads of your ill‑omened foes be cut off.'
segments.s139-g71: چو is 'when'. انباز is an obscure/archaic noun (probably a personal name or a rare term) and is not transparent in modern glosses; here I mark it as uncertain. او is the 3rd‑person pronoun 'his/he'. گشت is past 'became/turned / came'. با او is 'with him'. براز is a noun 'dung/manure' — the clause states that when 'انباز' arrived/turned up he came with dung (i.e. in a shameful/insulting manner).
segments.s139-g72: ببود is an archaic past copula 'was' (common in epic diction). آن شب 'that night' is the subject. تیره 'dark/ominous', دیر 'long (of time)', and دراز 'long/prolonged' are a triad of adjectives describing the night; word order places the copula before the subject in this archaic/poetic clause ('was that night dark, long and prolonged').
segments.s139-g89: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning 'when' (introducing the temporal clause). خورشید رخشنده شد is a clause with خورشید as subject and رخشنده a descriptive adjective; شد is the past copular/verb 'became/appeared' (here 'shone' / 'became radiant'). بر سپهر is a prepositional phrase 'over/on the sky' that locates the action.
segments.s139-g90: بیاراست is a past verb 'adorned' (bi·ārāstan). روی زمین را is the definite object ('the face/surface of the earth') marked by را. به مهر is an instrumental/benefactive phrase 'with affection / by kindness' (به used here with the sense 'by/with').
segments.s139-g91: به پدرود کردن is a verbal-noun phrase 'to perform a greeting' (به + پدرود + کردن). گرفتش is the past verb 'he took him' with the clitic object -ش ('him'). به بر is a prepositional phrase meaning 'into his arms/into his embrace' (به + بر).
segments.s139-g92: بسی here functions as an intensifier 'much' / 'many' (classical Persian). بوسه دادش is 'gave him kisses' — دادش is past 'gave' with enclitic object -ش. به چشم و به سر are prepositional phrases 'on the eyes and on the head' specifying where the kisses were given.
segments.s139-g93: پری چهره is a compound noun 'fair-faced maiden / beautiful woman' (پری used as epithet + چهره 'face'). گریان is a participial adjective 'weeping' modifying her. ازو is the prepositional pronoun 'from him/away from him'. بازگشت is the past verb 'returned/withdrew' (here 'withdrew' fits the context).
segments.s139-g94: This clause predicates a change/state with گشت 'became/turned'. ابا and انده appear as nouns/adjectival elements indicating reluctance or refusal and a placed sorrow (classical/poetic usages); درد is 'sorrow / pain' and انباز is an archaic/uncertain modifier. Grammatically, these items form the compound predicate 'she became (گشت) reluctant (ابا) and sorrowful (درد ... انباز)'. (Note: انباز is an obsolete/uncertain word in the text and its precise nuance is unclear.)
segments.s139-g95: بر رستم آمد is a verb phrase 'came to/approached Rostam' (بر used here with the sense 'toward/to'). گرانمایه شاه is an epithet-appositive 'the esteemed / honored king' identifying the subject who performed the action.
segments.s139-g96: بپرسیدش is past 'he asked him' with the clitic -ش. از introduces the content of the question with the sense 'about/concerning'. خواب و آرامگاه are the two objects of inquiry: 'sleep' (here meaning rest/sleep) and 'resting place' (آرامگاه as 'place of rest').
segments.s139-g57: بفرمود is an honorific/past finite verb meaning ‘he ordered/said’. تا introduces purpose ('so that / in order that'). موبدی پرهنر is a noun phrase 'a mobad (Zoroastrian priest) skilled/cultured' (noun + adjective), placed after the particle تا as the subject of the subordinate purpose clause.
segments.s139-g58: بیاید is a jussive/subjunctive third‑person (literary) meaning 'may come / should come'. بخواهد is the subordinate verb meaning 'to wish / to ask' (here 'to request'); ورا is the object 'her/it', and از پدر is the source phrase 'from (her) father'. The two verbs combine as 'may come to request her from her father.'
segments.s139-g59: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید is the past tense 'he heard' (third person). The sequence places the verb before the subject (typical in epic diction): 'When heard the king these words, he became glad' — شاد شد is a predicative adjective + auxiliary meaning 'became glad'.
segments.s139-g60: بسان introduces a simile 'like/as'. یکی سرو is a pictorial simile 'a single/ lone cypress' (یکی functioning as 'one/a'). آزاد شد is 'became آزاد' — here poetic: 'stood free/proud/noble'; شد is the past auxiliary 'became'.
segments.s139-g61: بدان پهلوان is an indirect object 'to that hero' (بدان = به + آن, an archaic/deliberate classical form meaning 'to that'). داد is the simple past 'gave'. آن دخت خویش is the direct object 'that daughter of his' (آن = that; دخت = daughter; خویش + ش = his/own), so the clause literally reads 'to-that hero gave that daughter his.'
segments.s139-g62: بدان سان is another archaic prepositional phrase 'in that manner' (ب + دان + سان = به آن سان). که is the relative particle 'that/which'. بودست is an older/poetic past form meaning 'it was' (compound of بود + است in classical style). آیین و کیش are parallel nouns 'ritual/custom and creed' — the clause means 'in the manner that was the custom and the creed.'
segments.s139-g63: به governs an instrument/means phrase 'by/with'. خشنودی و رای و فرمان list the items 'approval/pleasure, counsel/opinion, and command' joined by و. اوی is the archaic third‑person possessive/pronoun 'his' (here indicating these were his approval, counsel, and command).
segments.s139-g64: به خوبی is an adverbial phrase 'well/properly'. بیاراست is a past/telic verb 'he arranged/adjusted/ornamented' (here 'set in order'). پیمان اوی is the direct object 'his pledge/covenant' (پیمان = pledge; اوی = his), so the whole says 'he properly arranged his pledge.'
segments.s139-g49: مگر here is a rhetorical interrogative/conditional particle (used like “is there…?” or “could it be that…?”) rather than the literal ‘except’. چو means ‘like/as’ and introduces the comparison ‘like you’. بودن (باشد) is here an optative/subjunctive sense (‘there be’/‘exist’) rather than an imperative; the phrase به مردی و زور uses به + abstract nouns to mark the quality/realm (‘in manliness and strength’). تو is the poetic ‘thou/you’. Word order: the comparative phrase ('چون تو') precedes the verb 'باشد', and the qualities follow with به.
segments.s139-g50: سپهرش is سپهر (‘sky, heavens’) with the 3rd‑person possessive suffix -ش; here it functions as the heavenly power/fortune related to the person. دهد is a present/jussive form of ‘to give’ used optatively (‘may give’/‘would give’). بهره is ‘a share/portion’. کیوان and هور are celestial/ornamental referents (astral names/qualities) that describe the kind of portion granted. The order places the possessor/subject before the verb and the object (بهره) after it.
segments.s139-g51: سه دیگر = ‘three more/three others’ (numeral + 'دیگر'). که introduces a subordinate/relative clause ('that/if/which') here with the sense of a conditional/relative. اسپت is اسپ (steed) with the 2nd‑person suffix -ت (‘thy steed’). به جای آوردن is an idiom: به جای آوردن = ‘to put (into) place, to set right/ready’; آورم is 1st‑person present/subjunctive (‘I bring/set’), used here to express intent or potential action.
segments.s139-g52: سمنگان (Samangan) is a place‑name and the patient/goal of the boast. همه = ‘all, the whole’. زیر پای آوردن is an idiom meaning ‘to bring under one’s feet’ (i.e. to subjugate or trample). آورم again is 1st‑person present/subjunctive or intentional (‘I will bring’/‘I will make’). The whole line expresses a boastful conditional: if I do X, I will subdue Y.
segments.s139-g53: چو is a temporal conjunction here = ‘when’. رستم is the subject. برانسان is the noun meaning a cloak/mantle (برانس) — ‘upon the mantle’ — and is placed between subject and object as a circumstantial element. پری چهره is a compounded epithet (‘fairy‑faced’/‘beautiful of face’) describing the person seen. دید is simple past ‘saw’. The order is typical Old/poetic Persian (temporal marker → subject → circumstantial → object/epithet → verb).
segments.s139-g54: ز here is the poetic preposition ‘from’/‘of’ indicating source. هر دانشی = ‘every kind of knowledge’ (هر = each/every, دانشی = knowledge). نزد او literally ‘in/near her’ (نزد used locatively = ‘with/in the presence of’), here meaning ‘in her/within her’. بهره دید is a light idiom: ‘to see/receive a share’ — i.e. to possess or show a portion of that quality. دید is past tense (‘he perceived’).
segments.s139-g55: و دیگر = ‘and another’ (introducing an additional person/case). که is the relative particle ‘who/that’. از + رخش = ‘from Rakhsh’ (رخش is the steed’s name; here a source). داد آگهی is a verbal phrase: آگهی دادن = ‘to give information/notice’; here داد (gave) + آگهی (information) produce the sense ‘received/gave tidings’ depending on voice — in context it reads as ‘who obtained/received news concerning Rakhsh’ (classical word order places the noun after the verb). The construction and ordering are characteristic of poetic diction.
segments.s139-g56: ندید is the negative past ‘did not see’. ایچ is an archaic/poetic word meaning ‘nothing, not at all’. فرجام = ‘end/outcome/fate’. جز is ‘except’. فرهی is an adjective/noun meaning ‘noble, cultured’ (here rendered as ‘nobility’/‘glory’). The clause therefore states that this person ‘saw no outcome except nobility’ — i.e. the only result he perceived was honor.
segments.s139-g9: This is a poetic apposition: 'دو ابرو کمان' = 'two brows (that are) bows' where the noun ابرو is followed by a metaphorical noun کمان acting as a descriptive apposition (no copula). The coordination 'و دو گیسو کمند' repeats the pattern: two locks of hair described as کمند (a coil/lasso-like shape). Word order is noun + metaphorical descriptor, typical of Persian poetic compression.
segments.s139-g10: 'به بالا' is an adverbial phrase meaning 'upward' or 'raised up' (به + بالا). 'به کردار' is a simile-marker: کردار here functions as 'in the manner/behavior' (i.e. 'in the manner of'). 'سرو بلند' is a noun (سرو) followed by its adjective (بلند), 'a tall cypress'. There is no finite verb; the whole phrase modifies/finishes the prior descriptive line.
segments.s139-g11: روانش (روان + -ش possessive) = 'his spirit/soul'. خرد is used adjectivally ('wise' or 'of wisdom'). بود is the past copula 'was' and attaches to the preceding nominal phrase. The following nouns (تن جان) share the same adjectival predicate (پاک) with the copula understood from بود (ellipsis of repeated بود is common).
segments.s139-g12: تو گفتی که = 'you said that' (تو + گفتی as past 'you said'; که introduces the reported clause). بهره ندارد ز خاک = 'has no portion/share of the earth' — بهره 'share', ندارد 'does not have' (present/past sense depending on narrative), ز is the poetic form of از meaning 'from/of', and خاک 'earth/dust'. The clause states the reported judgement about the person described previously.
segments.s139-g13: از او = 'because of/at sight of her' (prepositional cause). رستم شیردل is the name plus epithet in apposition. خیره ماند is a compound verbal expression: خیره (staring/astonished) + ماند (past of ماندن 'to remain/be left') meaning 'was left staring' or 'stood amazed'.
segments.s139-g14: This phrase describes his immediate action: بر او can mean 'toward/over her' (direction/focus). بر جهان‌آفرین را بخواند = he 'read/uttered/called (the name of) the Creator' with را marking the definite object جهان‌آفرین. بخواند is past tense meaning here 'called upon' or 'pronounced/invoked'. The first بر marks direction (to/at her), the second بر takes the sense 'on/upon' or is simply part of the pictorial phrase 'uttered over her'.
segments.s139-g15: بپرسید is past 'he asked' (third person). زو = ز + او, the poetic contraction meaning 'from her'. گفت introduces direct speech 'he said' (in older/narrative style the verbs of asking/speaking can appear in sequence: 'he asked, from her he said ...'). The clause that follows is the direct question 'نام تو چیست؟' where نام تو = 'your name' and چیست = 'what is'.
segments.s139-g16: چه جویی = 'what are you seeking?' where چه is interrogative 'what' and جویی is a present/archaic 2nd-person-singular form of 'جوئیدن/جستن' (to seek) meaning 'you seek'. شب تیره = 'dark night' (شب + postpositive adjective تیره). کام تو چیست؟ is a second question 'what is your desire?' with کام 'desire' and the copula چیست 'what is'.
segments.s139-g25: This is a prepositional/adverbial phrase. به here functions adverbially ('in the manner of' / 'as'), followed by the noun-phrase کردار افسانه where the ezāfe that would normally link the two is dropped for poetic diction: 'کردار افسانه' = 'the manner/way of a tale.' از هر کسی is a following prepositional phrase 'from any/various people' that will serve as the source for the verb in the next segment.
segments.s139-g26: شنیدم is a first-person past tense verb 'I heard' (literal 'I have heard'). همی is an archaic/poetic adverb here indicating repeated or ongoing report ('often'/'frequently') and adds emphasis. داستانت is the object 'your tale/story' (داستان + enclitic -ت 'your'), and بسی is a post-positive adverb meaning 'much/many' (emphatic amount). Together: 'I have often heard many tales of you.'
segments.s139-g27: که introduces a subordinate/relative clause ('that...'). از marks the relation of fear ('afraid of') and governs a coordinated list of threats joined by و: شیر و دیو و نهنگ و پلنگ = 'lion and demon and whale and leopard.' This whole phrase is the oblique object of the verb نترسی in the next segment.
segments.s139-g28: نترسی is the 2nd-person singular present (classical) meaning 'you do not fear' and takes the preceding از‑phrase as its object. و simply links to the following predicate. هستی here functions as copula 'you are' (present 2nd person) and introduces the predicate چنین تیزچنگ, where چنین 'such/so' qualifies the compound adjective تیزچنگ ('sharp‑clawed' used poetically).
segments.s139-g29: This is a temporal/setting phrase followed by the verb. شب تیره تنها = 'a dark, lonely night' (adjectives follow the noun). به + توران + شوی: به marks direction/inclusion ('into/to Turan'), and شوی is the 2nd-person present/subjunctive form of شدن/شدن ('become/go' in older poetic use) giving an optative/irrealis sense 'you may go / you go'. The sequence places the temporal phrase before the verb as typical in Persian.
segments.s139-g30: بگردی is the subjunctive/potential form (ب‑ + verb) here meaning 'you may roam / you wander'; it governs the locative phrase بر آن مرز ('upon/along that border'). و هم links an additional predicate: نغنوی = ن + غنوی, where the initial ن is the negative prefix and غنوی (from غنود) means 'to fall asleep.' So the whole clause reads 'you roam that border and (yet) do not sleep.'
segments.s139-g31: به here marks the beneficiary/target ('for' / 'on account of'). تنها یکی = 'only one / a single person' (تنها as 'only' modifying یکی). گور بریان is a poetic compound 'scorched/roasted grave' (بریان qualifies گور), and کنی is the 2nd-person present/subjunctive 'you would do / you make' (optative/conditional flavor). The clause is a vivid, figurative statement: for one person you would alone cause a grave to be 'roasted' (figuratively devastated).
segments.s139-g32: هوا را is the definite/object-marked noun 'the air / the atmosphere' (را marks it as the direct object). به شمشیر uses به instrumentally ('with/by the sword'), where به is functioning adverbially/agentively. گریـان is a participial/adjectival form meaning 'causing to weep' and together with کنی ('you cause') forms a causative: 'you make the air weep with your sword' — a poetic way of saying your blade brings sorrow or a mournful effect.
segments.s139-g41: بجستم is a first-person perfect/preterite (archaic literary form) of the root جستن 'to spring/raise oneself', here 'I sprang'. همی is an emphatic/continuous particle common in classical Persian (adds vividness). کفت و یال are two coordinated nouns ('palm/hoof/cheek or paw' and 'mane'); they act as the instruments/parts involved in the action — i.e. 'with (my) paw and mane'. برت is بر + تُ (on/against you), a prepositional phrase attached to the verb, so the whole chunk means roughly 'I leapt (and) with paw and mane upon you'.
segments.s139-g42: بدین شهر کرد contains کرد (third-person past of کردن 'to make/do') governing the phrase بدین شهر (بدین = 'to/into this' or 'with this', شهر = 'city'); the clause reads 'made into this city'. ایزد is the nominative 'God/Divinity' (the agent) and آبشخورت is a possessive compound (آبشخور + ‑ت 'your') meaning 'your watering-place' or trough; overall a blessing/statement: '(May) God make your watering-place into this city.'
segments.s139-g43: ترا‌ام is an older clitic combination meaning 'I am (for) you' / 'I am yours' (ترا + ام). کنون‌گر is a compressed conditional phrase (کنون 'now' + گر 'if') meaning 'if now' or 'if at present'. بخواهی is second-person present/subjunctive of خواستن 'to wish/to want' ('if you want'), and مرا is the object pronoun 'me'. Together they form a protasis: 'If you now want me / if you would have me now.'
segments.s139-g44: نبیند is a negative third-person present ('(no one) will see' or 'does not see'); Persian often omits an explicit universal subject so it means 'none will see'. جزین is 'except these' (جز + این). مرغ و ماهی are a coordinated nominal phrase 'bird and fish' and مرا is the object 'me' (here: 'see me'); the clause says 'no one will see me except this bird and my fish.'
segments.s139-g45: یکی here introduces 'one [reason]'; آنک is a relative/introductory particle ('that which' / 'that'), after which بر تو ('toward you') gives the target and چنین 'thus/so' qualifies the change. گشته‌ام is the perfect of گشتن 'to become/turn' in first person singular: 'I have become.' The clause literally: 'One (reason) is that I have become thus toward you.'
segments.s139-g46: خرد را uses را as the definite/object marker ('wisdom' as direct object). ز بهر is an archaic prepositional sequence meaning 'for the sake of' (ز = from/because of; بهر = for). هوا here means 'desire/passion'. کشته‌ام is the first-person perfect 'I have killed' (classical/poetic perfect of کشتن). The clause: 'I have killed wisdom for the sake of desire.'
segments.s139-g47: ودیگر 'and another' introduces a second reason. که is the subordinating particle 'that'/'who'. از تو here means 'because of you' or 'from you' (a causal/pertinence sense). مگر in this context functions as a speculative particle meaning roughly 'perhaps'/'might it be that' (classical usage often carries a tentative/rhetorical sense). کردگار is 'the Creator/God' as subject of the following verb. The clause frames a conjecture: 'And another (reason) is that, because of you, perhaps the Creator ...'.
segments.s139-g48: نشاند is an archaic past of نشاندن / نشانیدن here meaning 'set/placed' (third-person past). یکی پورم = 'one (son) of mine' (یکی = one/a certain; پورم = پور + ‑م 'my son'). اندر کنار is a locative phrase 'at/into the side/beside' (i.e. 'beside [you]'). So the clause means '(he) placed one son of mine beside (you)'.
segments.s139-g81: ور is the archaic conditional 'if' introducing the clause. ایدونک is a temporal/particle (poetic) modifying آید, here giving a sense of 'now/at this time/soon'. آید is 3rd-person present (narrative/conditional) 'comes'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. اختر + پسر is the noun phrase 'a son from the star' with the head noun پسر ('son') following its modifier اختر ('star').
segments.s139-g82: ببندش is an imperative with the clitic -ش 'him/it' (bind him/fasten him). ببازو functions as an adverbial/prepositional phrase 'by the arm' (ب = به + بازو), placed after the verb. نشان پدر is a noun phrase 'a token/sign for the father' where نشان is the noun 'token/sign' and پدر is the possessor/beneficiary in genitive relation.
segments.s139-g83: به بالای سام is a locative prepositional phrase 'on/at the height of Sam' (به + بالای + proper name). نریمان is the subject placed before the copula, and بود is the past copula 'was'. The order is poetic/inverted (locative phrase + subject + verb).
segments.s139-g84: به here marks a quality or state ('in/with'), so به مردی means 'in manliness' (i.e. he was manly). و links the two predicates. خوی کریمان is a genitive phrase 'the disposition/character of the generous' (خوی = nature/disposition; کریمان = 'the generous'). بود again is the past copula 'was', so the whole clause says he possessed manliness and the character of the generous.
segments.s139-g85: فرود آرد is a verbal phrase: فرود 'descent/alighting' with the verb آرد 'brings' (poetic third-person). از ابر is the source 'from the cloud(s)'. پران is a postpositive adjective 'winged/feathered' modifying عقاب ('eagle'), so the noun phrase reads 'a winged eagle'. Word order is poetic (verb then source phrase then modified noun).
segments.s139-g86: نتابد is the negative present-third form of تافتن ('to shine/strike'), here 'does not shine/does not strike'. به تندی is an adverbial 'with swiftness/quickly' and بر او آفتاب is the prepositional object 'the sun upon him' (بر = on/upon, او = him, آفتاب = sun). The clause means 'the sun does not (even) strike/shine upon him quickly.'
segments.s139-g87: همی بود is a frequentative/progressive past construction (همی + past copula) meaning 'it was / it kept being'. آن شب is the time phrase 'that night'. بر ماه روی is a locative/visual phrase 'on the moon's face' (بر = on, ماه = moon, روی = face/surface); note the poetic word order and omission of connective markers common in Ferdowsi.
segments.s139-g88: همی گفت again uses the iterative particle همی before گفت to mean 'he kept saying / he continued to speak'. از هر سخن marks the content 'about every word/subject' (از = about/concerning, هر = every, سخن = speech/word). پیش اوی is a prepositional phrase 'in his presence / before him' where پیش = before/in front of and اوی is an old-form pronoun referring to 'him'.

sentences.s139-s49: When this was said, he gave him the good tidings, 'Go to Rakhsh'; the heart of the crown-giver became joyful.
sentences.s139-s50: He came, rubbed (it) and put the saddle on; Rakhsh grew radiant and the king was glad.
sentences.s139-s17: Whoever sees your mace in hand will rend the lion's heart and the leopard's claw.
sentences.s139-s18: When the eagle sees your unsheathed sword, it will not hasten to seize it as prey.
sentences.s139-s19: Hezbar bears the mark of your lasso; from fear of your spear the clouds rain blood.
sentences.s139-s20: When I heard these tales from you, I often bit my lip because of you.
sentences.s139-s1: When a portion of the dark night had passed, the nightingale turned upon the revolving sky.
sentences.s139-s2: Words came, hidden in secrecy; in the sleeping-place they gently opened (it) again.
sentences.s139-s3: A certain servant, a candle-stand in hand, came stately to the bedside of the drunk.
sentences.s139-s4: Then, behind the curtain, a moon-faced one — like the shining sun — full of color and scent.
sentences.s139-s37: When the shining sun from the lofty sky was about to cast a radiant snare.
sentences.s139-s38: On Rostam's arm there was a single amulet, which was famous throughout the world.
sentences.s139-s39: He gave it to him and said, 'Keep this, if fortune brings you a daughter.'
sentences.s139-s40: Take it and sew it onto her tresses, under a good star and with an omen that lights the world.
sentences.s139-s9: She answered thus: "I am Tahmineh; you say (it) as if grief has split me in two."
sentences.s139-s10: I am a daughter of the king of Samangan; from the line of Hezbar and of the leopards I am.
sentences.s139-s11: In the world there is no match for me among the fair; as for one like me under the blue sky, there are few.
sentences.s139-s12: No one has seen me come out from behind the veil, nor has anyone ever heard my voice.
sentences.s139-s33: When they entrusted the daughter to that hero, everyone — old and young — became joyful.
sentences.s139-s34: Out of joy they showered (scattered) much gold; they cried 'Bravo, hero!'.
sentences.s139-s35: ‘May this new moon be auspicious for you; may the heads of your ill‑omened enemies be torn off.’
sentences.s139-s36: When Anbāz came to him with dung, that night was dark, long, and prolonged.
sentences.s139-s45: When the radiant sun shone over the sky, it adorned the face of the earth with affection.
sentences.s139-s46: He began to greet him and took him in his arms; he gave him many kisses on the eyes and on the head.
sentences.s139-s47: The fair-faced maiden, weeping, withdrew from him; she departed, reluctant and full of sorrow.
sentences.s139-s48: The honored king came up to Rostam and asked him about his sleep and resting place.
sentences.s139-s29: He ordered that a skilled mobad be brought so that he might ask for her from her father.
sentences.s139-s30: When the king heard these words he became glad, and like a lone cypress stood proudly.
sentences.s139-s31: He gave that daughter of his to that hero.
sentences.s139-s32: With his consent, counsel, and command, he duly arranged his pledge.
sentences.s139-s25: Is there anyone like you in manhood and might, to whom the heavens would give the portion of Kayvān and Hūr?
sentences.s139-s26: Three more — if I set thy steed in its place — I will bring the whole of Samangan beneath my feet.
sentences.s139-s27: When Rostam saw the fairy‑faced maiden upon the mantle, he perceived in her a share of every kind of knowledge.
sentences.s139-s28: And another — who received news from Rakhsh — saw no outcome but nobility.
sentences.s139-s5: Two eyebrows like bows and two locks of hair like coils, raised upward like a tall cypress.
sentences.s139-s6: His spirit was wise; his body and life were pure.
sentences.s139-s7: At sight of her, Rostam the lion‑hearted stood stunned; he called upon the Creator.
sentences.s139-s8: He asked her, and she said: 'What is your name?' 'What do you seek this dark night? What is your desire?'
sentences.s139-s13: I have heard many tales of you from all sorts of people, told like legends.
sentences.s139-s14: that you fear neither lion, demon, whale, nor leopard, and are thus sharp‑clawed.
sentences.s139-s15: On a dark, lonely night you may go into Turan; you will roam along that frontier and yet not sleep.
sentences.s139-s16: For the sake of a single person you alone would scorch a grave; with your sword you would make the air weep.
sentences.s139-s21: I sprang forward and struck (at) you with paw and mane; may God turn your watering-place into this city.
sentences.s139-s22: If you would have me now, none will see me except this bird and my fish.
sentences.s139-s23: One [reason] is that I have become thus toward you; I have slain wisdom for the sake of desire.
sentences.s139-s24: And another [reason] is that, because of you, perhaps the Creator set one son of mine at (your) side.
sentences.s139-s41: If now a son comes from the star, bind him by the arm and make him a token for his father.
sentences.s139-s42: On Sam's height stood Nariman; he was a manly man and had the nature of the generous.
sentences.s139-s43: He brings down a winged eagle from the cloud; the sun does not quickly shine upon him.
sentences.s139-s44: All that night the moon's face was (there) and he kept speaking of every topic before him.
